content: We Work Remotely: Remote jobs in design, programming, marketing and more https://weworkremotely.com/remote-jobs.rss We Work Remotely: Remote jobs in design, programming, marketing and more en-US 60 LawnStarter: Senior Product Manager, Pricing Anywhere in the World Product Full-Time <img src="https://we-work-remotely.imgix.net/logos/0171/5881/logo.gif?ixlib=rails-4.0.0&w=50&h=50&dpr=2&fit=fill&auto=compress" /> <p> <strong>Headquarters:</strong> United States <br /><strong>URL:</strong> <a href="http://lawnstarter.com">http://lawnstarter.com</a> </p> <p><strong>About LawnStarter</strong></p> <p>LawnStarter is the nation's leading on-demand marketplace for lawn care and outdoor services, with over $100M in annual bookings. We're expanding beyond lawn care to become the one-stop shop for all home services — operating across three brands (LawnStarter, Lawn Love, Home Gnome) on a single shared platform.</p> <p><strong>About Pricing at LawnStarter</strong></p> <p>Upfront pricing is our competitive moat. Most home service marketplaces make you find a Pro and wait for a custom quote. LawnStarter gives customers a price immediately and assigns a Pro. That's a huge differentiator — but it means we have to get pricing right at scale for services where the industry norm is custom quotes for everything.</p> <p>That tension — seamless upfront pricing vs. inherently custom work — is what makes this domain both critical and uniquely challenging. Today, pricing is split across three fragmented systems with no dedicated owner.</p> <p><strong>Requirements</strong></p> <p><strong>The Role</strong></p> <p>You'll own the full pricing and monetization domain — from the infrastructure that powers every price we show to the strategy that determines what we charge, how we bundle, and where we expand.</p> <p>This is a transformation role. We're migrating from three disconnected pricing paradigms to a unified dynamic pricing system. The roadmap has 11 priorities, a dedicated engineering team, and no full-time product owner.</p> <p><strong>What makes this role different:</strong></p> <ul> <li> <strong>You own the entire pricing domain</strong>: Not a slice of pricing alongside other PM work. Pricing infrastructure, dynamic pricing strategy, new revenue models, service availability — all yours.</li> <li> <strong>Strong data team partnership</strong>: A data team owns pricing models and analytics. You define what to optimize. They figure out how. You don't build models — but you speak the language fluently.</li> <li> <strong>Infrastructure-first sequencing</strong>: The big pricing gains require a new pricing API and data product first. You need to be the PM who gets energized by building the foundation, not frustrated by it.</li> </ul> <p></p> <p><strong>What You'll Own</strong></p> <ul> <li> <strong>Dynamic pricing roadmap</strong>: Migrate three legacy pricing systems to a unified pricing service. Sequence the 11-priority roadmap, make tradeoff calls, and ship.</li> <li> <strong>Pricing strategy</strong>: Define pricing for 24+ services across mowing, non-mowing, and emerging verticals. Balance conversion, margin, and Pro economics.</li> <li> <strong>New revenue models</strong>: Unlock bundles, add-ons (e.g., "bag my clippings"), channel discounts, and frequency-based pricing — none of which exist today.</li> <li> <strong>Service availability</strong>: Determine where and when we offer services based on supply, demand, and profitability signals.</li> <li> <strong>Experimentation framework</strong>: Stand up A/B testing for pricing changes, measuring impact on conversion, margin, and Pro claim rates.</li> </ul> <p></p> <p><strong>Problems to Solve</strong></p> <p><strong>Three pricing systems that can't talk to each other.</strong>&nbsp;Pre-priced tables (1.8M+ rows of static lookups), instant quote logic (hardcoded per-service rules across APIs), and manual Pro quotes. None can combine location + frequency + brand + supply-demand into one decision. You'll architect the migration to a unified system without breaking pricing that 100K+ customers rely on today.</p> <p><strong>Mowing is 90%+ of revenue and stuck on static pricing.</strong>&nbsp;Our biggest service can't apply dynamic variables like supply tightness, seasonal demand, or channel discounts. A $3 price change swings conversion by ~10%. You'll partner with the data team to build pricing intelligence into mowing without destabilizing the core business.</p> <p><strong>24+ services need to migrate, each one different.</strong>&nbsp;Bush trimming, pool cleaning, landscaping, leaf removal — different pricing variables, ordering flows, customer expectations. You'll define the migration sequence and determine which services get dynamic pricing vs. simplified models.</p> <p><strong>No bundles or add-ons exist.</strong>&nbsp;Customers can't bundle services for a discount or add options to their mowing — a major untapped revenue and retention opportunity. You'll design the pricing architecture that makes bundling possible.</p> <p></p> <p><strong>What Success Looks Like (Year 1)</strong></p> <ul> <li> <strong>Pricing API/service shipped and live</strong>&nbsp;— Replaces at least one legacy paradigm with a clear path to consolidating the others</li> <li> <strong>Mowing on dynamic pricing</strong>&nbsp;— Core service running on the new system with measurable margin or conversion impact</li> <li> <strong>Experimentation framework operational</strong>&nbsp;— Team can A/B test pricing changes and measure impact within days, not months</li> <li> <strong>Bundle/add-on architecture defined</strong>&nbsp;— System design complete and engineering building, even if not yet live</li> </ul> <p></p> <p><strong>Who You Are</strong></p> <p><strong>AI-native.</strong>&nbsp;You use AI daily — scenario modeling, pricing analysis, data exploration, drafting specs. You push AI into parts of your workflow others haven't thought of yet. This is unlikely to be a good fit if you view AI as a novelty rather than a core productivity lever.</p> <p><strong>A systems thinker who architects platforms, not just sets prices.</strong>&nbsp;You see pricing as a system: inputs, rules, feedback loops, edge cases. You can design a pricing architecture that handles 24 services across 3 brands with different economics — and explain it to an engineer in a way they can build. This is unlikely to be a good fit if your pricing experience is limited to spreadsheets or optimizing a single product's price point.</p> <p><strong>Data-informed, not data-dependent.</strong>&nbsp;You partner with the data team to define what to optimize and interpret results. You know when the data is insufficient and a judgment call is needed. This is unlikely to be a good fit if you either ignore data or refuse to move without perfect information.</p> <p><strong>Technically fluent.</strong>&nbsp;You work directly with engineers on API design, discuss schema tradeoffs, and review technical design docs with real feedback. You don't write code, but you ea
